summary: Gruber offers his first impressions of Apple's iPhone SDK announcement, covering enterprise support, developer tools, the App Store model, and gaming potential. He admits he was wrong about Apple pursuing enterprise features, praises the developer tools as exceeding expectations, and analyzes the 30/70 revenue split as acceptable to developers despite being steep. He raises prescient questions about how App Store exclusivity will be technically enforced and whether EDGE network restrictions on apps like VOIP can be practically adjudicated. He predicts the App Store will trigger a gold rush driven by convenience, exposure, and volume sales.
key_points: Apple's enterprise iPhone support was unexpected — Gruber admits he was wrong to doubt Apple would pursue this market
The developer tools exceeded expectations with Interface Builder support, a desktop simulator, and the new 'Cocoa Touch' API, all Mac-only — potentially drawing Windows developers to the platform
The App Store's 30/70 split is steep but developers accept it for the convenience and massive exposure of reaching every iPhone user with one-click purchasing
The closed App Store model raises unanswered questions about technical enforcement — if developers can sideload their own apps, what stops users from doing the same?
EDGE network restrictions on bandwidth-heavy apps like VOIP seem difficult to enforce once apps are in the wild
money_quotes: There was never any question that this is a big market, the question was whether Apple wanted a part of it.
This has the potential to do for Windows developers what the iPod did for Windows users — move them to the Mac.
The unique control options — no traditional buttons but a 3D accelerometer and multi-touch screen — make the iPhone analogous to the Wii, in that it opens up new concepts in game UI design.
"70%? That's… that's… livable," which seems to sum up the consensus sentiment.
There is no option to circumvent the App Store.
What developers lose per-transaction from Apple's 30 percent take, they can more than make up for in volume. This is going to be a gold rush.
I suspect it's intended as a bozo filter to keep the process from being inundated with glorified do-little "Hello World" apps.
key_insight: The iPhone SDK and App Store represent a transformative trade-off: developers surrender distribution control and a 30% cut in exchange for frictionless purchasing and exposure to every iPhone user on Earth — a deal that will spark a gold rush.
